data.title: Suncor Energy: Leaves Some To Be Desired
data.description:
Brief Thesis Walkthrough

If you have read any of the articles I’ve made over the past couple of weeks, I think you’d find an undeniable pattern that I am bullish on the energy sector. Some of my top buys are ONEOK (OKE) and Energy Transfer (ET) right now, and you can read my opinions more clearly here and here. Despite Suncor Energy (NYSE:SU) being a significant player in the North American oil industry, its current valuation and dividend yield leaves some to be desired, unfortunately.

Compared to its peers it falls short in some areas. It's projected to only slightly grow production in 2025. SU also trades at a premium both on book value and FWD cash flows. I won't go too harshly on the stock, but I will not be making a buy unless I can get a better entry point.

SU Market Position And Current Market Climate

SU is an oil and gas company with its primary focus being on exploration and production of assets in the Oil Sands basin in Canada. Along with this SU also works with offshore operations of exploration and production on the East Coast of Canada. Finally, SU also works with the refinement and distribution of crude oil. These operations are divided into three segments in the financial reporting of SU:

Oil Sands

Exploration and Production

Refining and Marketing

The majority of the assets that SU owns are located in Canada, its only certain refining assets which are located in the US.

Looking closer at the Oil Sands assets SU owns I can see that they’ve performed very well in the past quarters and reached a record utilization rate of 99% during Q4. The financial reporting for the fourth and final quarter was released on February 5, 2025, and has since resulted in a higher stock price, up 3.3%. SU notes in its quarterly presentation that upgrader utilization has been realized through transformative steps taken during 2024. Looking at the Capex between 2023 and 2024 there was little change, going from $4.48 billion to $4.5 billion.

Out of this budget, SU dedicated $3.04 billion to its Oil Sands assets. From the 2023 annual report, SU spent $3.87 billion in Capex for its Oil Sands assets in total, the majority going to the base assets which was $1.97 billion, $1.3 billion in asset maintenance, and $627 million in economic investments. It seems that previous investments in SUs Oil Sands assets are now starting to pay off with higher utilization rates. YoY production here was up 7.9% to 817 mbbls/d for the quarter.

Given these production levels, SU is actually one of Canada's largest energy companies and one of the largest operators in the Oil Sands basin. According to Investopedia SU ranked as the country's fourth largest energy company. SU actually beats out another company I have covered on Seeking Alpha before called Cenovus Energy (CVE). In that coverage, I concluded CVE to be a buy based on solid asset performance in just the Oil Sands basin.

Oil Sands Basin

The Oil Sands basin is the single most important area of oil production right now in Canada. During 2023 it was responsible for 58% of the country's total oil production. The region also has one of the world's largest reserves, putting Canada behind both Saudi America and Venezuela in this figure. In the basin, there are some key differences to other well-known basins like the Permian for example. The Oil Sands are very rich in something called Bitumen. This is an oil that is too thick to flow on its own and in colder temperatures, it can be as hard as a hockey puck. In SUs financial reporting they actually differentiate the two “varieties”. In Q4 SU had Bitumen production at 342 mbbls/d, up QoQ from 294 mbbls/d. Extracting Bitumen companies use “in situ” methods. About 80% of all Oil Sands deposits are in situ, and the remaining 20% are utilizing surface mining instead.

Since 2010 Bitumen has made up a substantially larger part of the total hydrocarbon production in Canada and it continues to rise. According to the Alberta Energy Regulator, Canadian Bitumen production will increase from 3411.1 103 bbl/d in 2023 to 3953.7 103 bbl/d by 2033 representing an increase of 15.9% or 1.59% annually.

As for the current market climate, it can be described as uncertain but with overblown fears regarding Canadian tariffs from Trump. Energy exports into the US from Canada will face a 10% tariff, which will be paid by the importer. Given the large demand in the US, I don't necessarily think this will impact SU in a very negative manner. The company will be able to continue exporting everything that it wants and with already high EBITDA margins of 34.45%, its resilience will shine through in the coming years.

Looking ahead, prices of Canadian crude oil are expected to rise in 2025 and beyond says Alberta Energy Regulator. During 2024 it averaged $61.5 and in 2025 is expected to rise by 4% to $64. By 2033 it's forecasted to grow an additional 14.8% to $70.63. Assuming an across-the-board 4% increase of WCS in 2025 SU could be looking at $12.744 billion in EBITDA.

Management Comments And Outlook

“A fact for you, every major asset company-wide, upstream and downstream, operated at greater than 100% utilization for the entire fourth quarter. This is extraordinary performance and a credit to Suncor teams company-wide.”

Quote taken from the Q4 Earnings Call held on February 6, 2025, by President and CEO Rich Kruger. The quote in my mind covers how SU has had a fantastic 2024 in terms of operational performance. Given the strong asset performance, the TTM ROA is now at 6.7%. Compared to a 5-year average of 4.4% the capital divestments that SU has made towards Oil Sands have been paying off big time. SU is now closer to CVE in terms of ROA which has also had a fantastic 2024 with high utilization levels.

“If you do the math, organizational self-help, what Suncor teams delivered in volumes, cost, margins added $2.75 billion in free funds flow year-on-year, essentially offsetting the headwinds dollar for dollar.”

A leading reason for the rise of SUs stock price following the financial reporting was the amount of additional free fund flows that SU managed to accumulate during 2024. $2.75 billion more is not a small figure. When we look backward this figure actually would be more than enough to cover the company's dividend during 2024, which was $1.94 billion. SU has guided $3.3 billion in additional free funds flow growth by 2026. SU states this will be achieved through outperforming historical production levels along with a strong capital commitment, or in stronger discipline for investments. This would bring the total FFF figure to over $7.5 billion.

Cash Returns Reinforced By Robust Balance Sheet

At the time of writing the FWD dividend yield for SU is 3.99%. In 2024 SU spent $1.94 billion on paying a dividend and $2 billion on buying back shares. This brought total cash returns for investors to $3.94 billion. In relation to the current market cap that is 3.94%. SU generated $12.2 billion in EBITDA during 2024, meaning 32% was returned to shareholders.

SU states that all excess funds will be diverted to share buybacks. This is possible because SU achieved its net debt target of $8 billion in Q3 2024. This enabled the business to add additional ash returns for investors. Over the long-term SU is committed to a 3 - 5% annual dividend growth as well. In 2025 SU estimates $6.1 - $6.3 billion in Capex, with a majority going towards asset sustainment and maintenance.

2025 Financial Projections

Cash From Operations - $11.54 billion (4% above 2024)

Capex - $6.2 billion (Includes Economic Capital)

Dividend Payout - $2.01 billion (4% Midpoint increase from SU guidance)

Short-Term Debt Obligations - $1.1 billion (Current Portion of LT Debt + Current Portion of Lease Obligations)

Total Excess Funds - $2.23 billion

If all the excess funds are utilized to buy back shares it would reduce the total outstanding amount by 4.54% based on a $39.9 average share price. Modeling this return with the dividend yield of 4.02% investors are looking at 8.56% in 2025.

Why SU can do these large cash returns to investors comes down to the fact it has a solid balance sheet with little leverage. SU holds $2.4 billion in cash and $9.9 billion in current assets. LT debts are valued at $6.5 billion and as earlier mentioned the short-term obligations are $1.1 billion. SU can cover short-term obligations with its cash reserve but given the significant EBITDA SU generates it can use this income instead of tampering with the balance sheet. EBITDA to net debt is 1.57. This coverage is solid and significantly de-risks SU in the short-term and medium-term assuming no significant drop in commodity prices which would affect EBITDA.

Peer Group Comparison And Fair Value Assessment

In my fair value assessment, I’ve chosen to compare SU to another big Oil Sands operator, CVE. If you’ve read the coverage I did on CVE you’ll know the outcome of my comparison already.

Key Financial Figures And Multiples

Market Cap

SU - $49 billion

CVE - $27 billion

EBITDA / Net Debt

SU - 1.57

CVE - 1.4

FWD EV/EBITDA

SU - 5.02

CVE - 4.64

FWD P/B

SU - 1.53

CVE - 1.35

2024 ROA

SU - 6.7%

CVE - 6.84%

FWD Dividend Yield

SU - 3.99%

CVE - 3.89%

From the figures I’ve shared, I stand by my point that SU is a hold rather than a buy now. Over the past 3 years shares of CVE are down 2.1% and shares of SU are up 30.85%. Why I still make CVE the buy and not SU is that there is far more immediate upside potential for the stock. CVE trades at a lower EV/EBITDA than SU has a lower FWD book value and a higher ROA. Despite SU's excellent performance in 2024, CVE has still outperformed in my opinion. It's a matter of time before the market realizes. If CVE gets a bump on EV/EBITDA to 6x it's a 22% upside, not accounting for the 3.89% yield on cost by investing now. For SU, the upside is 16%.

Risks To The Thesis

The primary risk to my hold rating is that SU manages to boost production and output in 2025 beyond my estimates. This would mean investors who held off buying shares would miss out on potential higher returns. However, with a hold rating, I am also taking into consideration simply not adding to a position if there is an existing one. This still leaves exposure to the upside and investors can take part in the expected 8.56% cash returns to market cap I have for 2025.

Falling oil prices would affect the core earnings of the business and if persistent the FWD EV/EBITDA would rise potentially leading to a correction to keep the multiple around the 5x mark. Most Canadian oil companies have solid break-even points, estimated to be at the $50 mark. SU has taken measures to reduce its exposure to falling oil prices and in its Corporate Guidance for 2025 announced it aims to reduce the WTI break-even point by $10 compared to 2023. At the end of 2023, the WTI breakeven was $53 for SU. SU achieved a net reduction of $7 meaning that in 2025 SU is well on its way to achieving the $10 target.

I include this in the risk segment since a failure to actually get this number down in time would like to rattle the market.

Conclusion

SU had a great 2024, but the stock is not offering enough upside value right now. Cash returns promising going into 2025, but it still leaves some to be desired. Larger companies in the energy sector, like ET, for example, offer greater growth outlooks and deliver a better dividend. Because of the pressure Canadian oil companies are facing, the rising tensions between the country and the US will persist. Pessimism over the industry could drag down the stock price of SU to its 52-week lows around $32 - $34 per share, at which point the shares would look a lot more attractive to buy. For the moment though, I have SU on hold.

data.title: Eversource Energy: Newly Minted Dividend Aristocrat
data.description:
Durable income streams are built brick by brick, and for those income investors who have patience and fortitude to stick it out, the rewards along the way can be highly gratifying. Utility companies can fit this mold and be great portfolio diversifiers and stabilizers for an income portfolio.

This brings me to Eversource Energy (NYSE:ES), which is a newly minted S&P 500 (SPY) Dividend Aristocrat that’s given investors durable dividend raises for 25 years. In this article, I highlight Eversource including recent business results and forward outlook, and discuss why income investors may want to consider layering into this stock at present for potentially strong returns. Let’s dive in!

Why ES?

Eversource Energy is a leading energy utility based in New England. It serves over 4 million customers across the states of Connecticut, Massachusetts, and New Hampshire. Eversource’s three business segments are involved in the distribution of electric transmission, natural gas, and water.

ES is focused on generating reliable cash flows as it’s a 100% regulated utility, and makes investments that are in line with state policies while focusing on customer reliability and affordability. The three states in which it operates have relatively stable population bases which are seeing increasing demand for electricity. This could mean a long runway for growth, as New England’s electric demand is expected to more than double by 2050. As shown below, ES has delivered reliable returns for investors over the past 3 years, with a 6% CAGR in both the EPS and dividends per share.

Meanwhile, ES delivered strong 2024 results, with adjusted EPS growing by 5.3% YoY to $4.57. This was driven by growth in all three of Eversource’s segments. Electric distribution grew by 1.7% YoY to $1.77 due to base distribution rate increases for the Massachusetts and New Hampshire businesses, partially offset by higher O&M (operating and maintenance), interest expense, and depreciation.

Also encouraging, the Natural Gas Distribution segment grew earnings by 26.5% YoY to $0.81, due to higher base distribution rate increases in Massachusetts and continued investments in the gas system to replace aging infrastructure. Excluding the pending sale of Aquarion, the Water Distribution segment grew EPS by 33% to $0.12 due to lower depreciation.

Management is guiding for full year EPS of $4.75 at the midpoint of the range, representing 4% growth from the 2024 level. This is slightly lower than management’s long-term target of 5% to 7% annual growth due to equity dilution from a $1 billion ATM (at the market) equity raise in 2024 and the near-term earnings dilutive effect of the Aquarion sale.

Nonetheless, the Aquarion sale enables ES to focus more attention and resources toward its core electric and natural gas operations, not least of which includes grid modernization and replacement of aging infrastructure. This includes the recent strategic acquisition of the Mystic interconnection hub in Everett, Massachusetts, and the collaboration on the Cambridge Energy project (also in Massachusetts), which serve to meet growing demand in the region, as noted during the recent earnings call:

With [Mystic’s] strategic location, flexibility and existing infrastructure, this facility stands out as one of the most promising multiuse interconnection points for large-scale energy resources in New England, representing a unique opportunity to support the region's energy goals and spur economic development. This site will allow us to transform it into a premier energy interconnection hub that enhances reliability and energy supply diversity for the entire New England region.

Another great example is the Cambridge Energy project that recently broke ground in Kendall Square. This project, which is mostly a transmission investment led by Eversource, includes the construction of the first fully underground electrical substation in the United States. This 35K square foot substation will be located over 100 feet beneath a public park. It's an exciting development for the community in a significant step towards a more sustainable and resilient energy future.

Meanwhile, ES maintains a BBB+ rated balance sheet. This is supported by a reasonable near-term target FFO to debt ratio of 13% and expected proceeds from various business activities, including the $1.6 billion sale of the Aquarion water business, as shown below.

Importantly for income investors, ES is a Dividend Aristocrat with 25 consecutive years of raises under its belt. It currently yields 4.9%. The dividend is well-covered by a 62% payout ratio and comes with a 5-year CAGR of 6%.

Lastly, I find value in ES at the current price of $61.54 with a forward PE of 13.0, which sits well below its normal PE of 18.4, as shown below.

With key growth drivers coming from a renewed focus on electric distribution expansion, a near 5% dividend yield, and expectations for a long-term EPS growth rate of 6% annually, ES could reasonably deliver potential market-beating total returns from the current discounted valuation. Plus, any reversion to its mean valuation could be an added bonus.

Risks to the thesis include Eversource’s need to raise around $1.2 billion from equity issuances, which could be near-term dilutive. Plus, higher interest rates could lead to higher interest expense and pressure the bottom line. Regulatory uncertainty as it relates to base rates could lower Eversource’s profitability. ES also carries execution risks as it relates to potential project delays and cost overruns.

Investor Takeaway

Eversource Energy is a discounted Dividend Aristocrat that offers an attractive opportunity for income-focused investors. It has a demonstrated track record of delivering steady growth from regulated utilities with a strong market position in New England.

Eversource’s renewed strategic focus on electric and natural gas infrastructure, combined with grid modernization, positions it well for long-term growth. Its discounted valuation, near-5% yield, and long-term growth prospects make it an attractive option for investors looking to build a durable income stream with potential for market-beating total returns.

data.title: Lions Gate Q4's Big Story: The Library
data.description:
Lions Gate Entertainment (NYSE:LGF.A) (NYSE:LGF.B) is finally ready for its close-up, as they say - rather, make that its separation. According to CEO Jon Feltheimer during the company's recent Q4 earnings conference call (February 6, 2025), to my understanding that event should happen this April.

Anyone who has followed this story could be forgiven, though, for expressing cynical skepticism on that timeline, because it seems as if we've been promised the full and final separation for a long time. We did at least get the Lionsgate Studios (LION) stock via a SPAC transaction. The reason for the delay is sourced to some regulatory details.

I'll get into the numbers of the latest quarter in a moment, but upfront, the whole idea for LGF shareholders, the one thing that has constantly been at the forefront but has never materialized, is an acquisition of the company at a large premium. That's the speculation...and yes, I emphasize speculation.

I'll also highlight something from the conference call in this article - a great topic I tend to mention in many of my media articles. This one was tailor-made for me.

I have been a shareholder for a long time, and I am waiting to see how this all turns out. Many probably have not invested alongside me, and for obvious reasons, I can see why. The shares have acted more like a trading vehicle (which is understandable, given the eventual collapse of the A/B shares into one class and the premium for the A voting shares and the ups and downs of the slate).

I wrote about Lions Gate last back in June 2024, and the company was considered cheap then by the SA quant system; perhaps not so much now, as I will get to (but valuation is secondary to consolidation). It also continues to wait for movie hits to fuel its prospects.

But the biggest element of the story is the company's content library...that's where the attraction, and the multiple, will be, and that's how Feltheimer can at last pull the curtain down following the final act of this corporate show.

The Lion's Quarter - Roaring Earnings, Quiet Cash Flow

The studio beat on the top and bottom lines: adjusted $0.28 per diluted share for Q3 versus expectations of $0.08, sales of $970 million which was better by $50 million.

But, there was no growth. On a non-GAAP basis, $0.27 is what the company earned in last year's Q3. A whole penny better.

Furthermore, on a nine-month basis, there was a loss of $0.07 versus a profit of $0.44 last year at this time.

Moving to cash flow, there were only deficits. Besides GAAP cash flows, there is an adjusted free cash flow that the studio reports which takes into account tax credits and production loans (i.e., payments and borrowings).

For the quarter and the nine-month period, there were operational outflows of $120 million and $360 million, respectively. Both compared to surpluses in the previous frames.

Going to the adjusted stats, the quarter saw an inflow of $13 million, a sharp drop from the previous $60 million. The nine-month deficit in adjusted free cash was $200 million versus a surplus of similar size.

In other words, cash was basically nonexistent, especially if we just go by the GAAP figure (which arguably is the better approach).

The company will need to get a better hold of managing expenses over time. As well as some other stuff, which I will now discuss.

Thoughts On The Quarter And The Library

We all know that Lions Gate is now all about the separation from Starz and the ability for Feltheimer to use that as a way of selling out to the highest bidder.

But he doesn't have much of a film strategy going. So...what is the attraction here?

We all know the answer: the company's content library.

Perhaps the biggest driver of this stock's story - besides consolidation - is the multi-thousand title library. Over the last twelve months (the studio always reports trailing 12-month stats) the revenue from the library was over $950 million, which represented a record achievement for the company, and calculated out to over 20% growth year-over-year. Pretty soon one will expect a ten-digit number on the library, and that is, generally speaking, a high-margin influx of value.

It's impossible to know all the rights issues inherent in the library, but the assumption has to be that there is enough flexibility within this collective asset to allow all kinds of leverage opportunities.

For instance, remake rights must be on the table for a plethora of titles, as well as streaming/broadcast episodic adaptation. Add on sequel rights and video-game adaptations, and that's just the beginning of the potential here.

Additionally, the library will flourish now that many streamers are pivoting toward advertising. Lions Gate undoubtedly has many gems in its collection that would be attractive to streamers to license that would attract viewership and engagement, not merely sit there as something to take up server space and be discovered by accident -- they would be algorithmically-friendly, in other words.

Yet Lions Gate has to have a backup plan in case a buyer continues to be elusive. The company does have some big movie product coming up, such as a John Wick spinoff, but even then, there have been struggles with the movie slate. Cutting costs on production seems to be one answer toward this conundrum.

And this brings me to what I alluded to earlier about the conference call and a great question that was asked. Analyst Matthew Harrigan inquired about the cost of talent, artificial intelligence, and if anything has been learned via the Borderlands debacle (quick recap for those who are unaware: Borderlands, which was supposed to be a summer blockbuster, grossed $30 million worldwide from an August release date, and it cost $110+ million to make and tens of millions more to market, and it starred top talent such as Jamie Lee Curtis and Cate Blanchett and director Eli Roth):

[...] but are you seeing more rational behavior across all levels of talent for the industry on compensation? I guess a little cringe here, is there anything happening with AI that’s also helpful on the cost side? And then secondly, on Borderlands, I know you’ve tweaked your green lighting process somewhat. Could you give us a few specifics as to what the learnings were there and how the unfortunate experience could maybe even help you moving forward? And obviously, some of the cost savings might be the innovative marketing that you’ve always been good at, particularly on the social side."

Reading the entire answer is well worth it, but the bottom line on talent compensation is "I think talent is getting paid very well right now. I don’t know that necessarily that’s going down a whole lot...". That was from the CEO. There was further talk of increasing backend participation - an example given was the Tyler Perry co-production model - as a way of enticing participants to at least cut down the rest of the below-the-line costs (as well as, I suppose, an implication to reduce above-the-line costs for the talent that doesn't have as much power as the backend talent). But Feltheimer did remind us that, bottom line, and I quote, "[...] we can be smart overall about production costs, but as I say, talent expects to be paid." And by that he means, a whole lot. I guess it's good that the question was raised, but I'm not sure the company has a realistic strategy to reduce compensation practices for the top tier. Perhaps the backend model might work out if there are more hits in the movie portfolio.

There was also mention of learning from the Borderlands disaster, and maybe the company will, but until it embarks upon a commercially-focused, low-budget strategy, we may continue to see the slate struggle a bit.

Valuation/Conclusion

The LGF company/stock, classes A and B, owns a big chunk of LION plus the Starz streamer. For now, the company reports under both stock banners, so the LION portion could be basically thought of as something that tracks the studio portion minus the streamer.

The stock isn't particularly cheap, according to the SA valuation system. If you relate the enterprise value to sales (A shares), then it scores a decent rating (B- at time of this writing). But P/E, PEG, and metrics related to earnings and EBITDA/cash flow on a forward basis are in the D category. The B shares are about the same. Right now, there is a premium to the B shares since the A class represents more voting power. As mentioned before, eventually there will be one class once that is restructured as the conclusion of the separation nears.

It's difficult to say exactly when all this takes place. According to comments in the conference call, it is now supposed to be in April.

I own the company for its potential to be acquired. At this time, I also believe in the potential for the company to score theatrical hits, but its strategy is unclear to me in the sense that management didn't want to get into detail about it on the conference call, especially when asked about what it learned from the Borderlands failure. Adam Fogelson, the theatrical chairman, literally said he did not want to get specific about what the company took away from the low-return project. He just mentioned generalities about the right budget for the right movie, etc.

That's too bad; almost makes one think nothing was learned. But I do know that there are potential revenue-driving hits on the horizon with the aforementioned film set in the Wick universe, as well as Now You See Me later in the year and another Saw outing for Halloween. I'd rather have a stronger slate than that to be quite frank, but from a stock perspective, that could keep the momentum going post-separation and before some buyer steps forward.

Who would that buyer be? Considering how Paramount Global (PARA) fell into the hands of a tech scion, one has to wonder if private equity will hook up with someone from the tech world who wants to make Hollywood their new hobby. It's not necessarily a far-out idea.

There are risks to buying LGF or LION. Biggest two: the company's theatrical slump continuing indefinitely is one, while the lack of a buyer for the library is the other. If there is no buyer, then the company has to grow via successful content at theaters, it can't just be a supplier of television hits such as Ghosts.

The company's library could be worth at least $4 billion if one goes by the valuation for the MGM library. Plus there was about double that for the total acquisition if one includes all the goodwill spent to buy the studio. Considering how streamers are currently basing a lot of the value of their platforms on advertising-sales potential, it wouldn't be surprising for Lions Gate to have a takeout value of at least triple its market capitalization plus debt on the books.

But that's my assessment. It is not guaranteed to take place, hence a repeat warning of the risks in speculating with this equity.

The company has seen some momentum in its shares, so those who do want to speculate here may want to wait for pullbacks. Again, this is a risky investment idea, but for those who love to play around in Hollywood, it's one to look into further.

data.title: USXF: A High-Growth ESG ETF With Market-Beating Potential
data.description:
ETF Overview

iShares ESG Advanced MSCI USA ETF (NASDAQ:USXF) owns a portfolio of ESG screened U.S. stocks. The fund has an expense ratio of 0.10%. This is comparable to other ESG funds. For example, Vanguard ESG U.S. Stock ETF (ESGV) has a slightly lower expense ratio of 0.09%. USXF has a high exposure to growth and technology stocks. This has resulted in its outperformance to the S&P 500 index in the past and should improve its future growth outlook. However, this is a two-sided sword, as growth stocks are often expensive and USXF can be more vulnerable in market pullbacks. Given its potential for long-term outperformance to the broader market, USXF appears to be a good candidate to consider, especially for investors looking to integrate ESG principles into their portfolio.

Fund Analysis

USXF’s portfolio construction methodology

Let us begin by looking at how USXF constructs its portfolio. The fund basically seeks to track the investment results of the MSCI USA Choice ESG Screened Index. This index selects ESG-screened stocks from the MSCI USA Index. Companies that are involved in fossil fuel industry are excluded. The result is a portfolio of about 350 mostly large-cap and mid-cap stocks. Stocks are market-cap weighted, meaning higher market capitalization stocks will receive higher weights in USXF’s portfolio.

USXF has slightly outperformed the S&P 500

One of the main reasons of ESG investing is the belief that ESG-focused companies will often focus on sustainable innovation, and have lower regulatory risks. This should in theory result in better performance in the long run. We will now compare USXF with the S&P 500 index to see if the fund has indeed delivered better returns or not. As can be seen from the chart below, USXF delivered a total return of 114.2% since its inception in June 2020. In contrast, the S&P 500 index delivered a slightly inferior total return of 110.7%.

In the past five years, USXF has been through two bull markets. The bull market before 2022, and the current bull market that begun in October 2022. As the chart above suggests, in both bull markets, USXF has outperformed the S&P 500 index. As the chart below shows, in the current bull market that started on October 2022, USXF delivered a total return of 91.2%, outperforming the 76.8% total return of the S&P 500 index.

USXF is tilted towards growth stocks

Another main reason for USXF’s outperformance has to do with the fact that the fund has a high exposure towards growth stocks. As can be seen from USXF's stock style chart below, growth stocks represent about 35% of its portfolio, while value stocks only represent about 15% of the portfolio. This composition is very different from the S&P 500 index where growth stocks only represent 19% of the index and value stocks represents 27%. Since growth stocks usually have better growth profiles than value stocks, it is not surprising to see USXF outperforming the S&P 500 index in the past.

USXF’s exposure towards growth stocks is also a reflection of its exposure towards technology sector. As can be seen from USXF’s sector allocation table below, the fund has a 42.8% exposure to technology stocks. In contrast, technology stocks only represent about one-third of the index. As we know, technology sector has been the best performing sector among all other sectors in the past thanks to many technology innovations. Viewing from this perspective, it is not surprising to explain why USXF, with higher exposure to technology, has outperformed the S&P 500 index. Looking forward, the technology sector will continue to benefit from many technological megatrends such as artificial intelligence, cloud computing, robotics, industrial automation, etc. Therefore, it is likely that USXF will continue to outperform the S&P 500 index, given its higher exposure to the sector.

Readers may wonder why ESG-screened USXF has higher exposure to technology stocks. The reason is that many stocks from energy, basic materials, and utilities sectors are not included in USXF’s portfolio due to their involvement in fossil fuels. In addition, many mega-cap technology stocks such as Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), and Google (GOOGL) have higher ESG scores, and USXF’s market-cap weighted approach automatically means these technology stocks will have higher exposure in the fund’s portfolio.

Higher downside risk than the S&P 500

Investors should keep in mind that although USXF’s high exposure to technology and growth stocks will improve its growth profile, these stocks often trade at high valuations. Therefore, they may be more vulnerable in market corrections. As can be seen from the chart below, in market pullbacks, USXF has indeed declined more than the S&P 500 index in the past five years. Another factor that contributed to USXF's volatility relative to the S&P 500 index is the fund's inclusion of more mid-cap stocks. In fact, mid-cap stocks represent about 31% of USXF's portfolio. In contrast, the S&P 500 index's exposure to mid-cap stock is only 18%. Since mid-cap stocks tend to be more volatile than large-cap stocks, it is not surprising to see USXF to have higher volatility in market downturns.

Investor Takeaway

USXF’s ESG-screened methodology has resulted in its high exposure to growth and technology stocks. This has in turn resulted in its outperformance relative to the S&P 500 and improve its long-term growth outlook. The downside of this is its higher downside risk in market corrections. Nevertheless, USXF is still suitable for investors wishing to align their investing with ESG principles and at the same time seeking long-term outperformance.

Additional Disclosure: This is not financial advice and that all financial investments carry risks. Investors are expected to seek financial advice from professionals before making any investment.

data.title: HP: Q1 Earnings Preview, Sentiment Low With AI PCs A Potential Catalyst For Growth In 2025
data.description:
Introduction

HP Inc. (NYSE:HPQ) is in a bit of an interesting spot right now. With the End of Life date for Windows 10 being later this year in October and AI PCs finally looking like they are more than just a gimmick, it feels like HP should be in a prime position going into 2025. Unfortunately, it’s never that simple, especially when it comes to anything involving tech. Although I feel mostly positive about HPQ from both a short-term and long-term perspective, I feel that there are certain details and risks that need to be addressed before jumping in with both feet, especially with HP set to announce earnings later this month. Overall, I feel that a buy rating is appropriate for HP, albeit with a few caveats.

HP has been a leader in the PC manufacturing industry since the early 1970s, and since then, has expanded to provide computing and printing solutions at a consumer level, as well as other related products like peripherals. In 2024, HP’s net revenue from Personal Systems was $9.6 billion, which is up 2% year-over-year, although net revenue for FY24 was down 0.3%. Net revenue for HPQ has been on a downward trend since 2021 for HPQ, mostly as a result of declining revenue from personal systems (down 18% since 2021). However, we are currently reaching the end of the cycle for Windows 10, which means that personal systems should hopefully bounce back this year.

Underperforming Against Competitors

Over the past few years, HPQ’s momentum has slowed and become somewhat underwhelming compared to some of its peers. Below is a table listing several key statistics of HP compared to Dell (DELL) and Lenovo (OTCPK:LNVGY), two of HP’s biggest competitors, especially in the world of notebooks. HPQ is underwhelming in both Revenue Growth and Total Returns compared to its peers, although it does at least hold up well in units sold for 2024. Despite this though, I still feel that there are several compelling factors that make me feel optimistic about HPQ going forward.

Data collected from International Data Corporation (IDC), Seeking Alpha, and respective earnings reports

Market Misjudgment

HP is set to announce earnings towards the end of this month on the 27th, there are mixed signals on whether expectations will be met or not. HPQ has had 4 beats in the past 2 years. For the past two quarters though, HPQ missed estimates both times. As a result of this, I believe EPS revisions for this quarter are somewhat pessimistic, with the estimated EPS for Q1’25 being 0.74, the lowest quarterly EPS estimate it has had since 2021. I believe this overly negative estimate is setting the bar fairly low for HPQ. Not only this, but it's also worth noting that when Windows 7 reached end-of-life in January 2020, HPQ beat quarterly EPS estimates in all three of the following quarters. When Windows 8 reached end-of-life in January 2023, quarterly EPS estimates were beat in two out of three of the following quarters. This isn't to say that there is a direct correlation, but we may see this pattern continue with Windows 10 accounting for 60% of systems worldwide, which is more than Windows 7 and 8 combined at their end-of-life.

HPQ’s lackluster performance in recent memory has caused its valuation to trend towards being undervalued. Beyond this, it has also been trending towards being oversold for the first time since Displayed below we can not only see that HPQ is sitting right at the bottom of the displayed trend channel, but we also see that it is the lowest the slow STO indicator has been ahead of earnings being released since September 2022. We can also see that following this oversold status in September 2022, it rallied shortly after HPQ reported earnings thanks to beating quarterly EPS estimates. Furthermore, I think the market seems to have overreacted to HPQ’s lack of recent success, creating a dip that makes for a prime buying opportunity, mitigating several of the downsides I have previously mentioned.

AI PCs: Worth the Hype or Just a Gimmick?

One area that I want to draw attention to is the realm of AI PCs, which are a new generation of computers that have AI integrated into their hardware or software for a better user experience. If you were to ask me prior to CES 2025, I would’ve told you not to believe the hype around AI PCs just yet, it was still far too early to tell whether they would actually be worth anything. But now that companies like Dell, Microsoft (MSFT), and Lenovo have their announcements and reveals regarding AI PCs, the potential for this new technology is becoming more clear. HP is no different, unveiling its next generation of AI-powered notebooks. These announcements come at just the right time too, with Windows 10 officially set to no longer receive security updates beyond October 14, 2025, we will likely see a small surge in revenue generated by PC sales at the consumer level in 2025.

I believe that even though PC sales in 2024 were somewhat disappointing, a potential increase in demand for AI PCs, as well as the demand that will be created by the end of support for Windows 10, will allow HP to bounce back in this area. It will be important to keep an eye on the general reception of AI PCs though, as I said, it’s still too early to say for sure how they will affect the landscape of the industry and whether they will truly be as innovative as they are currently being described. PCs with AI features will likely become the standard in the next few years, displayed below is a forecast from Canalys which projects that AI-capable PCs will account for 60% of PC shipments in 2027. Whether the growth of the AI PC market will truly be this fast remains to be seen, but due to the ever-changing nature of AI and how fast we’ve already seen it develop and change entire industries, I would not be surprised if it had extreme effects on personal computing going forward.

Assessing the Risks

As I’ve mentioned several times, there are risks associated with HPQ that deter some of my optimism regarding its future. Most notably, underperformance and stagnation in recent years make it less attractive than it was a few years ago, but there are also inherent risks with any tech stock that must be mentioned. It will be important to keep an eye on rising tensions between the U.S. and China, considering the amount of manufacturing that companies rely on China for. It should be noted, however, that since August of last year, HP has reportedly been making efforts to move production out of China and expand their production in Mexico, Thailand, and Singapore. As a result, they are less at risk when it comes to a potential trade war between the U.S. and China. There is also always the risk of the hype around AI beginning to slow down, it feels like this has been said a billion times in just the past few months, but it should always be mentioned when it comes to stocks that have relied on AI for growth. In HP’s case, although I wouldn’t call myself a full-fledged believer in what AI PCs have to offer just yet. I think whether we like it or not, AI is here to stay in the PC manufacturing industry for the foreseeable future, whether it will be as transformative as it is currently projected to be is still uncertain though.

Conclusion

Overall, while there are several risks currently present, I still feel confident about recommending HPQ. I believe 2025 should be a good year for PC manufacturers, especially later into the year as consumers begin to replace systems that are not Windows 11 compatible with Windows 10 end-of-life approaching. I think that HPQ is an intriguing prospect from both a long-term and short-term perspective, and while it may not be as impressive as some of its competitors, 2025 should be a strong year. Due to the volatility the stock will face with Earnings approaching, I encourage you to do your own research if it interests you.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CNYA: Will DeepSeek And Tariffs Alter China's Competitiveness?
data.description:
Investment thesis

It has been over half a year since we last covered BlackRock’s iShares MSCI China A ETF (BATS:CNYA). A lot has happened since then.

Most publicly listed companies in China only report their Q4 and FY results in March. Therefore, we do not have fresh data on their earnings. By extrapolating what we saw in Q3, we expect an average earnings growth to be in line with the 5% growth to the country’s GDP.

In this article, we want to focus on key items that might have the largest impact on the development of the stock market in China.

First, let us start by looking at how the ETF CNYA has performed.

Performance of CNYA

The share price of CNYA did grow by a respectable 14.5% over the last 12 months.

CNYA and SPY should be seen as barometers for the stock markets of the world's two largest economies. The gap between the Chinese and the American market is not that large over one year. However, when we compare the two over five years, the S&P 500 is up 81% and CNY is up only 7%.

Here are topics we think are important to the development of China's economy and the companies' share prices.

DeepSeek

One reason the American “Magnificent Seven” did so well last year, was all the well-deserved attention they got around AI. It spilled into the whole market.

It will certainly change many ways in which we live and work. This development was led by innovation in the U.S.

When we wrote the conclusion to our previous article on CNYA, we did not know then that the development of AI would come from China this quickly. We were mostly thinking of all the benefits it will have on their industries.

We are no expert on this matter. However, in an article in SCMP in Hong Kong, titled “How AI start-up DeepSeek upended industry assumptions and changed China’s narrative”, we learned that DeepSeek’s release of reasoning model and large language model offers rivaling performance to OpenAI’s offerings at a fraction of the cost with similar quality.

The important lesson from what is taking place is to not underestimate China’s ability to innovate and bring to market technologies and products that are, to quote a Singapore minister;

“Cheaper, Faster, and Better”

Manufacturing and export

On the topic of faster, never have we seen so fast action from any government, as what we are now seeking coming out of the U.S.

As of writing, the U.S. has imposed a tariff of 25% on the import of all goods from Canada and Mexico. With China, it is a 15% import tax. In addition, they have also stated that there will be an import tax of 25% on steel and aluminum from Mexico, Canada, and the EU. These are to come into effect from the 12th of March.

These are supposedly based on reciprocity. On the other hand, it was said the tariffs are there to stop fentanyl coming into the U.S. through these countries. Or maybe it is both. It is hard to tell.

Europe will also be hit.

China has hit back with a 15% import tax on LNG and coal and 10% on crude oil. Exactly where these tariffs will end might change, as they are also seen as a negotiating tool.

We do not think the tariffs on Chinese-made goods will reduce the volume. Manufacturers in China are already selling goods with low-profit margins. In our opinion, it will be the consumers in the U.S. and Europe, which will have to bear most of the increase in the sale prices.

In our last article, we covered the influx of Chinese-made EVs flooding the global market. It was particularly in the EU, which does have a large automotive industry, where this became a concern. They raised tariffs of up to 37.6% effective from the 5th of July 2024.

The latest number from the General Administration of Customs in China, we can see that the drop in the export of EVs has been considerable.

It was our belief last summer that these setbacks to China’s export of EVs would be temporary. We still hold the same view. However, we would like to add some context to that statement.

Companies like BYD Company Ltd. (OTCPK:BYDDF) (OTCPK:BYDDY), which is now one of the top 10 holdings of CNYA, is still a “rookie”, when you compare it against century-old companies like Ford Motor Company (F) and Toyota Motor (TM). It will continue to grow, both domestically in China, and on a global scale. Temporary setbacks are inevitable.

According to a publication from the State Council of PRC, total exports in 2024 from China amounted to RMB 25.45 trillion, a growth of 7.1% Y-o-Y. Import also grew by 2.3%, expanding to RMB 18.39 trillion.

The property market

The property sector used to account for 25% of the nation’s GDP. It has been important on so many levels.

The first level is each provincial government has relied on the sale of leases of land as their largest income provider. Secondly, the real estate developer's cost of building homes. With that comes myriads of middlemen involved in the transactions of properties, including the mortgagors.

Then you have the effect of the wealth creation, when it did happen, which trickled down to the economy through positive consumer sentiment.

According to data from the National Bureau of Statistics, as of last year, total new residential sales had halved since reaching a peak in 2021, dropping to RMB 9.7 trillion. There is still a large inventory of unsold residential properties from developers, but analysts in China do see some improvements in sales prices and volumes and believe a recovery will start in 2025.

Affordability is always an issue, and we therefore move on to the issue of the cost of capital.

The economy

According to the World Bank’s latest estimate at the end of last, China’s GDP grew by 4.9%, and they have projected it to come in slightly lower at 4.5% this year.

When we last analyzed CNYA, the 5-year rate for property mortgages was 3.95%. It is now 3.6%.

Contrary to most other OECD countries, China has not had a problem with inflation over the last ten years. The highest it went was in 2018 when it reached 2.9%. Last year, it was only 0.2%. The National Bureau of Statistics of China forecast for this year is an inflation of about 1.6%.

The urban unemployment rate in China is 5.1%.

However, youth unemployment has received quite a lot of attention. Two years ago, it was reported as 20%, which prompted the government to stop publishing it. They have now revised the definition of unemployment to exclude students, and since it covers the age group of 16-24, a large portion of this age group is studying. It has been falling gradually over the last few months, and according to SCMP, as of November of last year, it stood at 16.1%. It would be more of a concern if it was high for the age group 25-29 years of age, but this is 6.7%, only slightly higher than the total unemployment rate.

Risks and conclusion

We cannot rule out global economic slowdowns. Although, it does not seem to be very likely for 2025. The economy in the U.S. is strong. Europe will muddle through, as usual.

However, investing in China by foreign investors could still be muted.

If we revisit the fundamentals that we shared in July last year, we note that the valuations are somewhat higher. However, the gap between the two remains the same.

We are not too concerned about the tariffs from the U.S. and EU.

Chinese steel, which is the third-largest exporter to the U.S. could grow on the back of overcapacity in China and their competitive advantage. How long that would last is dependent on the mood in the White House and its relationship with China.

Uncertainties are now the “new normal”.

Nevertheless, we look beyond the usual presidential cycles that take place every four years in the U.S. Over time, China will most likely become less reliant on just two trading blocks.

Trade with countries participating in the Belt and Road Initiative now accounted for 50% of all of China’s trade in 2024. This will most likely continue to grow.

China will remain competitive and offer good value.

Based on valuation, we keep our Buy call for CNYA.

data.title: Brad Simpson: Challenging The Narrative Around Tariffs And Their Impact On Markets
data.description:
With current U.S. trade policy creating an atmosphere of uncertainty for investors, Brad Simpson, Chief Wealth Strategist at TD Wealth, discusses strategies to help find clarity in the markets and position your portfolio to mitigate risk.

Transcript

Kim Parlee: Concerns about trade have been weighing on global markets as investors consider the potential economic impact on corporate earnings. Well, my next guest is here to challenge the narrative of that impact and what it could mean for markets. Brad Simpson is Chief Wealth Strategist at TD Wealth, and he joins me now. Nice to have you here.

Brad Simpson: It's great to be here.

Kim Parlee: Let's start with talking a bit about the narrative that has been developing around trade. We hear about, of course, tariffs have been-- are going to be implemented on steel and aluminum-- and we're hearing about more could be coming. And the impact on the markets, though-- that is really what you're here to talk about. So tell me what-- tell me what we mean when people are talking about and a bit about what we actually see happening.

Brad Simpson: Ah, you know, I'd go as far as to say this is-- in 34 years, this is one of the most remarkable times in my whole career. And I would say it comes down to-- if you think about, especially if you took the period from February 1 to the 3-- it's a Saturday to the Monday when we were when we were first talking about the first tariffs. And the long story short is if I looked at those three days... the Saturday, I read about tariffs all day. Sunday, I had two conference calls. Sunday-- two conference calls. Read about it all day. Sunday morning-- or Monday morning, I got up at 3:45 in the morning. I did three conference calls about this. So it was about three or four hours in. I had two meetings. So before it was even 10:00, I was five hours into talking tariffs.

And the long story short is, the middle of the market being open and looking at it and looking on FactSet and looking on my Bloomberg terminal and looking at all the noise around it. And then checking out and seeing what others were saying and all the hyperbole around it. And the reality is that markets were very calm. And there was something missing. Like somehow in the middle of all this noise and what was going on in the market, and even like reading about what happened in the market that day afterwards and how it's been-- the reality is, is markets have been very calm. And that-- and so I wanted to frame that. So I brought a chart along to kind of try to look at it.

So the one on the left, basically what it's saying is-- I took February 1 and looked at the change of the market during the day. So really, there was a high-low movement of the S&P 500 between 75-- down 75 to 100 to 150 basis points.

And all in all, if you kind of look at the market going back to 2000 and think of how many things have happened since 2000 in the markets, markets have traded like they did on February 1st 10% of the time, meaning that this was not an extraordinary day in any way. It was hectic, but it was an ordinary day in markets.

The one on the right, what I'm showing is, is going back and looking at when the first tariffs were implemented between the United States and China in 2018. And markets were moving from zero to down 4% for about three months there. That's high volatility.

And what I'm trying to-- what I wanted to frame with this is to say that every year we have our national conference and our research conference, which is on January 20. And I put this slide up on the screen, and I said, is the pricing mechanism of the market broken?

And so when non-finance guys speak, we say that going out 6 to 12 months is the market's pricing what you think is going to happen. And what I'm saying, was it broken? Was I was saying that who doesn't know that there's going to be tariffs? And the reality is, that the market knows this.

And so, what's happened from 2018 to now is that the market's become less extreme. The noise around it has become more extreme. And there's an amazing dichotomy in that. And I think that to continue to build the frame this way is just to understand that really, that the pricing mechanism of the market is working very well.

I think that for investors, the best thing to do to start with is that-- almost everybody's become an expert in tariffs now.

Kim Parlee: Yeah. In the same way it happened with COVID, the same way it happened with everything before it. Yeah.

Brad Simpson: And my suggestion is to start out-- let's just use this as a base case. There's going to be tariffs, full stop. And if we use that as your base case, then it gives you a lot of understanding, is that every time you hear a different thing happen and different change, is to go, well no, wait a minute. I was expecting this to happen, and so is the market.

And then, that really turns the volume down, and it gets it back to really what our job is to do. And our job to do is to manage capital and allocate capital and grow and protect it. And the reality is, is there's lots of good things going on in the economy where we can do that.

Kim Parlee: Yeah. I was going to say it's fascinating to hear that, too. Because yeah, when you think about the advent of social media, everything is amplified. And so to see some facts to show that that's actually not the case in the markets is coming. So thank you. That's actually nice to see.

Let's talk a bit about, you know-- the bottom line is when we want to go from an asset allocation standpoint, maybe let's talk about what you think about in terms of the different assets. So, I don't know-- if we want to start with fixed income, and you can tell us what you're thinking on that front.

Brad Simpson: Sure. Well, let's-- even for that, if we took a step to go, how are we going to make those decisions? And so, our starting point is that ultimately when you're making decisions, you have to determine where you think the economy is going to go.

Because if you look over the long term, if you look over 15 years, what makes investment works is over 15 years it's almost impossible to lose money. And the reason for that is there's usually a couple economic cycles during that time. And so, the starting point is for us is to say, if we can figure out where we are in the economy, if you can come to terms with the first and say that there will be tariffs.

And then you can-- the second one, you go, I'm going to determine what's happening in the major industrialized nations, the ones with all the skin in the game. So what's happening in Canada? What's happening in the United States? What's happening in the eurozone? What's happening in China?

And if you look at it in those terms and say, if I can frame that. And so we actually come up with just one consistent framing to say, let's look at employment. Let's look at manufacturing. Let's look at what the service economy is doing. Let's look at what--

Kim Parlee: Taxation.

Brad Simpson: There's that. And then let's look at monetary policy, and how much liquidity and what interest rate levels are. And if we're looking at that consistently, and we're looking how that's changing and how that's evolving in a world that where we know that there is this ongoing change in the tariff, we can start having a pretty good range in each of those major trading nations. And really, those are the nations that make the world's market go around, the world's economy go around.

And if you can do that-- and for us it's-- and that's our approach to doing things, I think if you do it, then you start to come into the conclusions how you're going to allocate. And again, if you think about it, everything I'm saying here is coming down to, I want to cut out the noise.

Kim Parlee: Right.

Brad Simpson: Right? I want to-- remember, I always say that it's not what you think, it's how you think. And for us, we're just consistently going and coming back and looking at things in a consistent way so we can take that noise out and allocate it.

Kim Parlee: When we think about asset allocation, maybe take us through the different asset classes and what you're thinking. Fixed income right now is a modest underweight. Why?

Brad Simpson: Well, when you're looking at a concern that there could be an escalation of inflation again-- just a concern, not making a call there-- then really, what you're doing is you're investing for the coupon, for the interest rate return. We don't really think there's going to be a lot of interest rate volatility. So there's maybe some opportunity for capital gains. But for the most part, clip the coupon on your fixed income. It's a good coupon and still makes an awful lot of sense.

Kim Parlee: Let's get into equities, because I know you have a little bit more behind here. And I think-- and correct me-- it's modest overweight on equities. Take us through your thinking.

Brad Simpson: And I think I-- well, I know I brought a chart along for this, and it kind of hits at home with this, is that I think we all know that how much over the last few years that returns and equity markets were derived by technology.

And so, when we look at the economy today, we make this pronouncement. And we say, we're not talking about a soft landing anymore. If anything, we're not even talking about an early-stage economy. This economy has been moving so fast, we have an economy that we think is mid-stage. And so, if it's a mid-stage economy-- which we think it is-- we think it's a healthy one.

And in an environment like this is, one, what we're showing on the left here is saying that cap-weighted versus the equal-weighted-- the equal weight is those other 493 names. And lo and behold, we think there's lots of opportunity there. 14% to 16% are earnings growth expectations across sectors, and a very, very healthy mid-stage economy, typically when an equal-weight index does quite well.

The right, what we're looking at is showing manufacturing picking up. As manufacturing is picking up, what tends to do well is, small- and mid-cap companies tend to do well in an environment like that.

And what we're showing on the last one is looking at there so much so, saying that if we look at the earnings expectation of small-cap companies and mid-cap companies, they are actually much higher than our expectations for large-caps, which is the complete opposite of what we saw in 2022 and '23 and '24.

And so, with that, we think that the fears and concerns around concentrated equity markets, they're understandable. The reality is this is a market that's really broadening out. And we think it presents an awful lot of opportunity for investors.

Kim Parlee: You have another chart here I want to bring up, too, and tell me a bit about this one from an equity standpoint, why this is material.

Brad Simpson: Yeah. It's actually-- it's twofold. What we're looking at here is, is we're looking at the correlation between equities and stocks. So, how are they moving together? When you build a diversified--

Kim Parlee: And these are fixed incomes.

Brad Simpson: Actually, fixed. Excuse me. Yeah, yeah. So, long story short is you want your fixed income and equities to move in opposite directions. What we're showing here is that they're increasingly moving in the same direction. And so, as a diversifier, they're not as reliable as they used to be.

And so for us, is to say is really what our notion here is, is that we are an asset allocation team that believes an awful lot in building portfolios with different diversifiers. And so, what we're trying to say is this is an environment where, if you're concerned about fixed income not being as good of a diversifier-- which this is showing that-- you want to get that from other areas. You want to hedge. You want to use long, short, and market-neutral strategies.

And in an environment-- if you look at the equity environment right now, what I'm showing you is as more and more names are moving out, that creates more and more opportunities for you to go long some of those names, short some of the others. That's a very good diversifier. And I would say, like, where we are with concerns about volatility in the marketplace, very much a changing economic environment, this is a very good strategy and way to build a better diversified...

Kim Parlee: And an active strategy.

Brad Simpson: Yeah. And so this also shows that active management, even long only, is a good environment for that.

Kim Parlee: I've only got about a minute here, and I apologize, because alternatives-- you have a modest overweight; commodities-- modest underweight. And I'm just thinking, again, this is when you think about the diversification as well, too.

Brad Simpson: Totally. So, one, we like infrastructure. I think, look, we have an awful lot of buildout in infrastructure. I mean, if you look at anything going on in the world, you'll see that--

Kim Parlee: Data centers.

Brad Simpson: --your core plus, your data centers, for sure. But even like your electricity grid, utilities, all those pieces. And so, we like that. We like that an awful lot.

And commodities-- I mean, if you look at the uncertainty that we had in December, where both fixed income and equity markets were under a little bit of pressure, commodities were up. And in an uncertain geopolitical environment, commodities are an excellent diversifier in portfolios. And with the supercycle that we like as well, we think we continue to like those in portfolios as well.

Kim Parlee: 15 seconds. I'm looking at the note that I know you're about to put out. So, I will say, you have here how to not get lost. Tell me a bit about that.

Brad Simpson: Well, I think it comes really full circle for us is that-- and as we talked about-- in this world of such extreme opinions and such extreme positions, that I think it's imperative that you have a process for how you think, that you have a diversified portfolio. But a diversified portfolio that I think it goes well beyond just owning stocks and bonds, making sure that if you're using hedging strategies and using things like private equity and private debt, private credit, investments like that. Be consistent with it. Try to keep out a lot of that noise. This is a lot-- this is a much better environment than what you think.
